primary_diagnosis.name: Uncomplicated acute bacterial lower urinary tract infection of the bladder

differential.diagnosis: uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (acute cystitis)
differential.evidence_basis: Classic triad of dysuria, frequency and urgency of acute onset in a non-pregnant adult woman with no systemic features.
Must be actively excluded — its treatment and triage differ sharply. Screened for with fever/rigors, flank pain, systemic toxicity and vomiting.
Can cause external dysuria — made unlikely by absence of vaginal discharge/itch and symptoms localised to micturition.
Considered if dysuria persists after antibiotics or with risk factors; suprapubic symptoms favour bladder over isolated urethral involvement.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading diagnosis is a lower UTI, but pyelonephritis must be explicitly screened out before it can be treated as uncomplicated.

differential.diagnosis: uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (acute cystitis)
differential.evidence_basis: No fever, no rigors, no flank pain, no vomiting, tolerating oral intake — no features of upper-tract or systemic infection. Consistent with an uncomplicated lower UTI.
Screened negative for systemic toxicity and loin pain — upper-tract involvement not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the pyelonephritis red-flag screen is negative, the presentation is a straightforward uncomplicated lower UTI suitable for empiric oral antibiotics and telehealth/GP management.

differential.diagnosis: uncomplicated acute bacterial cystitis (non-pregnant adult woman)
differential.evidence_basis: Presumptive clinical diagnosis; on-site dipstick (leukocyte esterase + nitrites positive, sealed clinician finding) is confirmatory but not required to start empiric therapy. Definitive confirmation is urine culture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the diagnosis (no examination, no dipstick) — the correct posture is a presumptive diagnosis with empiric first-line therapy and a clear pyelonephritis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Costovertebral angle (loin) tenderness, flank/back pain, high fever or rigors — features of ascending pyelonephritis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes an uncomplicated lower UTI from pyelonephritis, which requires a longer/different antibiotic course and often in-person or hospital care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated ascending infection can progress to urosepsis.

red_flags.description: Nausea/vomiting or inability to tolerate oral fluids/medication
red_flags.clinical_significance: Inability to take oral antibiotics or maintain hydration is a criterion for escalation to IV therapy in an emergency department.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failed oral therapy and dehydration in an evolving upper-tract infection.

red_flags.description: Systemic sepsis features — confusion, rigors with high fever, marked tachycardia, feeling gravely unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Urosepsis is a time-critical emergency requiring ambulance transfer.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Septic shock if an ascending infection is not recognised.

red_flags.description: Visible blood in the urine (frank haematuria) that does not clear with treatment, or symptoms in a pregnant patient, a man, a child, or someone with a catheter or recurrent infections
red_flags.clinical_significance: These features make the UTI complicated, altering investigation, antibiotic choice and follow-up, and may indicate an alternative diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed complicated UTI or an underlying urological cause.

pathophysiology_summary: Ascending colonisation of the bladder by uropathogens (most commonly Escherichia coli from the perineal/faecal flora) triggers a mucosal inflammatory response of the bladder wall. Inflammation of the bladder and urethral mucosa produces dysuria, and detrusor irritability produces the frequency and urgency of small volumes with a persistent urge despite an empty bladder. Bacterial metabolism and pyuria make the urine cloudy and malodorous, and urease-related and nitrate-reducing organisms drive the positive nitrite result on dipstick. In an uncomplicated lower UTI the infection remains confined to the bladder; ascent to the renal pelvis (pyelonephritis) is heralded by loin pain, high fever, rigors and systemic toxicity, which are absent here. A short course of an appropriate oral antibiotic eradicates the organism; symptomatic relief comes from hydration and analgesia while the antibiotic takes effect.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage of a common, low-acuity condition
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being alarmed by the severity of the pain and the 'urgent after-hours' framing of the source note and escalating an uncomplicated lower UTI to an emergency department or ambulance. The correct disposition is telehealth/GP/pharmacist-led empiric treatment with a safety-net, not 000.

Treating dysuria as an uncomplicated UTI without explicitly asking about fever, rigors, flank pain and vomiting — the single screen that separates a benign lower UTI from a serious upper-tract infection.

Recommending or endorsing a urinary alkaliniser alongside nitrofurantoin — alkalinising the urine reduces nitrofurantoin's antibacterial activity. Symptomatic alkalinisers are better paired with a different agent, or avoided when nitrofurantoin is chosen.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Assuming 'uncomplicated' without checking the patient category
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Uncomplicated management applies to non-pregnant, non-catheterised adult women. Pregnancy, male sex, childhood, recurrence or a catheter make it complicated and change the plan — these must be checked, not assumed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Costovertebral angle (loin) tenderness on percussion (clinician examination)
Exact vital signs — blood pressure, heart rate, temperature — without a home device
On-site urine dipstick result (leukocyte esterase, nitrites)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None immediately — this is telehealth/GP-manageable. Provide a presumptive diagnosis, arrange access to a first-line oral antibiotic (telehealth prescription, or GP/pharmacist UTI pathway), give symptomatic and hygiene advice, and safety-net clearly for pyelonephritis features that would warrant same-day GP or emergency review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low clinical risk. The main errors are OVER-action (escalating a benign lower UTI to ED/000) or under-screening (missing evolving pyelonephritis by not asking about fever, flank pain and vomiting).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic — Acute cystitis in non-pregnant women (eTG, current)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Empiric first-line therapy for uncomplicated acute cystitis in non-pregnant women is nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim; routine urine culture and imaging are not required for a single uncomplicated episode.
